content.problemStatement:
Read problems statements in Russian hereThe Head Chef has a reporting hierarchy for his chefs which is basically a binary tree i.e. Each chef reports to exactly one chef and every chef can take reporting of atmost two chefs . In case two chefs report to the same chef they must report at different times . The time of reporting can have only two values : Morning or Evening ( denoted by M and E , respectively ) .The Head Chef himself is not part of the reporting structure . The Head Chef has found that a new restaurant in Byteland is opening and it is hiring a group of chefs from some existing restaurant . The new restaurant will basically make an offer to one of the chefs and all the chefs directly or indirectly reporting to the chef . The Head Chef is worried that the new restaurant may be taking away some of his chefs . The Head Chef knows that the new restaurant will not change the reporting structure of the chefs . The Head Chef also has access to the reporting structure of the new restaurant in terms of ages of Chefs  . The Head Chef wants to know whether there is a need to worry or not . He needs to worry if the new restaurant can possibly be targetting his chefs . 


In short given a tree ** T** and another tree **O** you need to tell whether ** O ** is a subtree of ** T ** or not . The node values of ** T ** or ** O ** need not be distinct as they represent the age of the Chef . Also , a subtree of a rooted tree includes a particular node and the entire tree structure below it . 

 Two trees A and B are considered same if age(A->root ) = age(B->root) and subtree rooted at A->left is same as subtree rooted at B->left and subtree rooted at A->right is same as subtree rooted B->right .
content.inputFormat: First line contains N, the number of nodes in the main tree. Each of the next N lines contains an integer representing the age of a chef. The next N-1 lines contain two integers A and B and a character C, where A is the senior chef, B is the reporting chef, and C denotes the time of reporting. The next line contains an integer Q, the number of queries. For each query, the first line contains M, the number of nodes in the subtree. Each of the next M lines contains an integer representing the age of a chef in the subtree. The next M-1 lines contain two integers A and B and a character C, where A is the chef who takes the reporting, B is the reporting chef, and C denotes the time of reporting.
content.outputFormat: For each query, print 'YES' if the subtree is a subtree of the main tree, and 'NO' otherwise.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first query has a subtree with nodes 1 and 2, but the main tree has a different structure. The second query has a subtree with nodes 1 and 2, and the main tree has the same structure.
The subtree is a subtree of the main tree.
The subtree is not a subtree of the main tree.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we first construct the main tree and the subtree using the given input. Then, we use a recursive function to check if the subtree is a subtree of the main tree. The function checks if the current node of the main tree matches the current node of the subtree, and if so, recursively checks the left and right children of both trees.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N * M) per query, where N is the number of nodes in the main tree and M is the number of nodes in the subtree. The total time complexity is O(Q * N * M), where Q is the number of queries.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + M), where N is the number of nodes in the main tree and M is the number of nodes in the subtree. The dominant structure is the recursive call stack.
